data.title: Thursday's Stock Market Price Action Says Stocks Want To Go Higher
data.description:
Thursday’s opening for stocks started from the pre-market, were way down, and we could have easily tested the recent lows and stayed there. Instead, it reversed direction. Normally, market commentators will come up with a ready explanation of the market’s resilience, though in my opinion, often inaccurate ones. This time, I did not find one commentator offering a cogent reason for the market turn.

A market rejection of a strong leg down with no obvious catalyst, even an erzats one, is very bullish indeed. Just the hint of a “seller’s strike” is a great tell that market participants have discounted the worst news. When that happens, stocks should re-rate higher. Let’s start with a chart of the S&P 500 ETF (SPY) for 3 months.

I am using the SPY because it’s a very good proxy for the actual S&P 500 index. We see a strong downtrend ending with an inverse “Head-and-Shoulder” formation. It is quite bullish, but lacks the granularity of what happened on Thursday. So here is the same chart, but using the candlestick style. Here is the same 1-year chart:

I rarely find the need to use a candlestick chart, but it is useful in this instance. We have the same inverse-head-and-shoulders. Here I want to add that Wednesday night we had a presidential address, and the hope was that we would get more info about when the Iran war ends. Even though we were in a downtrend the day before, and going into the close, we see a nice lift off the bottom. “Stocks had opened lower after President Trump vowed to "hit Iran hard" and "send them back to the Stone Age" in a national address that failed to provide a certain end to the US-Israeli war in Iran. The President's speech suggested the US would escalate its military operations before withdrawing from Iran in two to three weeks.” (cite) When market participants went unsoothed of their concern about war, the futures on April 2, early before the open was quite low. I thought the nascent rally, or at least the recent stalled retreat we’ve been having, wouldn’t stand a chance of holding. I was happily wrong.

The market bounces up with no easily identified reason

Then, as we see in that circle above, the major indexes - the SPX (but also the) NDX turned positive and stayed positive. It has been repeated to me numerous times, and in my experience, this is an important “tell” that the market has been so sharply discounting all the detractions to our economy - the endemic inflation, the soaring gasoline, stalled housing market, and the biggest unknown… the war has been weighed and measured. It’s not 100%, nothing is 100%, more economic bad news, and the weighing machine that is the stock market might need more of a discount. So, barring any additional bad news, the low of the above chart could be support. That could be the start of a nicely sustained rally. The low for Thursday in the Nasdaq Composite was 21,371. The Thursday low for the S&P 500 Index was 6475, the Thursday low for the Nasdaq-100 was 23513. The rebound off these lows and turning positive before the early end of the week ended by the Good Friday holiday. The reversal from negative to positive into the close, in my opinion, confirms that this market action is probably the start of a sustained rebound.

Then, there was the fantastic macroeconomic news from Friday

On Friday, we were treated to some wonderful news. March jobs report; US economy adds 178,000 jobs, unemployment rate falls back to 4.35 in a surprise turnaround (cite). All we've been hearing about is the plight of "low hire, and low fire" economy, meanwhile, a 178K in jobs when there is practically zero immigration, and rampant retirement of boomers, is a strong datapoint to the contrary. This is an economy that is creating jobs, despite the jump in productivity and all the "AI-driven layoff announcements (cite.)

Not only were the recent prior months of March jobs number was positive in contrast to February, but the January jobs number was revised sharply upward. It doesn’t stop there; average hourly wages are up 3.5% (cite). That means that even at 3% inflation, hourly workers are earning more. The objection would be that the jump in gasoline will cancel out those gains. This is true, but if you agree with Powell, we are looking through the current pop in oil prices as a goad to inflation.

One thing is sure, once again, the feared “Stagflation” boogeyman is being brought back up into the attic of overused declarations, along with the boy who “Called Wolf,” one too many times. You can’t have a growth scare with employment growing above trend, either. So what does this do for stocks? The notion that stock values can go higher by elevating the P/E ratios was not possible. Now, after years of rising, P/E ratios have hit an upper limit and have moderated for a time. With a rise in earnings motivating higher stock price could have a positive effect on stocks valuation.

Rising earnings should result in higher share prices

Earnings for Q1 are still expected to be higher, even with rising energy costs, and the latest estimates for Q2 indicate a 17% increase in earnings(cite). Even if you chop that to 14%, stocks are well undervalued. That could mean that stock P/Es could justify a higher valuation based on further earnings growth. Most likely a combination of a re-rating of P/E ratios, not to new highs, just a tad more valuation. The current forward P/E of the S&P 500 is 19.9, and for the S&P without the Magnificent 7 is 18.90 times. (cite see figure 12)

Jobs are plentiful, indicating economic growth

76K went to healthcare, which boosted the number by an additional 35K from Kaiser-Permanente returning from a strike. Other areas to note were growth in manufacturing, construction, and logistics. There are a lot of positives that we’ve ignored, all these March Jobs numbers, combined with Jay Powell looking past the rise in gasoline (cite), and so no rate cuts but no rate raises either. Current estimates for earnings are as high as 13 for Q1 and higher, also estimates for Q2 and all of ‘26 17.4%. Yes, most of the higher earning areas are in the growth areas like Info tech, but also aerospace defense, and biotech (cite). This last cite is a very nice report on how earnings are expected to go according to FactSet.

My investments

I didn’t splash around a lot of new investment money. I decided to stay hedged over the weekend via the inverse Nasdaq-100 ETF (SQQQ). This is the second time I've put this hedge on. If I am correct and this is the bottom, it is likely to be a messy bottom. That means that this low will be tested more than once. Maybe it doesn’t hold completely; there is a risk that we will have multiple tests of this level. It is my opinion that market participants are not at all bought into the notion of a rally right now. Paradoxically, I like that the market sentiment, in my eyes at least, the overall expectation of stock prices is decidedly lower. You’ve heard the saying, “The stock market rises against a wall of worry.” Well, there are plenty of things to worry about. I did make some small investments, though I was mostly looking to hold onto my cash.

Stocks I acquired in small amounts: ATI (ATI), ATI joins my investments in Howmet (HWM), and Carpenter (CRS). These stocks make high-performance alloys for space and aviation applications. Several years ago, we identified that aerospace and defense would be a growth area. Tactically, we’ve embraced large-cap names along with small and microcaps in this sector. I have been building a position in DoorDash (DASH) to pair with Uber Technologies (UBER). I think internet-based service companies are still doing very well with the upper “K” consumer. Though, as we have seen with the reversal in perception of employment growth, the lower “K” consumer is still in the game. Even as I am still ready to start new positions, and adding to old ones, make sure to prioritize cash, and deploy it when the low of 6300ish is tested again. My hedge stays on until I see the test of the low, if, as I expect, the rally sustains, I will close out the hedge. Other than that, have a great week.

data.title: Fiserv: Deeply Undervalued, But Don't Expect A Sharp Rebound
data.description:
In the last few years, I have written about several companies from the Transaction Processing Service Segment. This includes companies like PayPal (PYPL), Visa (V) or Mastercard (MA). One of the major companies from this segment is Fiserv, Inc. (FISV) - a stock I never covered before.

But especially in the last few quarters, Fiserv suddenly became very interesting although in a rather negative way. The stock, which was still performing well at a point when stocks like PayPal or Adyen (ADYEY) already struggled, also declined steeply in the last few quarters. Following a brutal 2025, Fiserv is trading 77% below its previous all-time high - the steepest decline at least since the early 1990s.

In the following article, I will argue why Fiserv is not necessarily a company with a wide economic moat around its business, but trading at such low valuation multiples that it is hard to overlook as an investment opportunity.

Fiserv Business Model

As always, when analyzing a business for the first time, I will start with a brief business description. If you are already familiar with the business model, you might skip this section.

Fiserv was founded in 1984 and already had its IPO on the Nasdaq within two years after creating the business. Today, the company has about 38,000 employees and the company is offering payments and financial services solutions. The business can be split up in two business segments - the Merchant Solutions segment and the Financial Solutions segment. While the Merchant Solutions segment is offering mobile payment services as well as security and fraud protection solutions to mostly small-and- mid-size businesses, the Financial Solutions segment provides services for banks and financial institutions. This segment is providing digital payments as well as debit card processing services, fraud protection products, bill payment or person-to-person payments.

Similar to other businesses, Fiserv was also growing by acquisitions in the last few years. Especially noteworthy is the acquisition of First Data Corporation in 2019 for $22 billion, which had a huge impact on the business (as we will see later) - including margins, RoIC or shares outstanding.

Fiscal 2025 Results

Fiserv was certainly a business growing with a high pace in the last decades, but the lastest results were rather disappointing. On February 10, 2026, the company reported fourth quarter and full-year results for fiscal 2025. When looking at full-year results, revenue increased from $20,456 million in fiscal 2024 to $21,193 million in fiscal 2025 - resulting in 3.6% year-over-year growth. Adjusted revenue (which adjusts for postage reimbursements) increased 3.6% year-over-year from $19,123 million in the previous year to $19,804 million in fiscal 2025. While revenue still increased, operating income declined slightly (1.0% YoY) from $5,879 million in fiscal 2024 to $5,818 million in fiscal 2025. Diluted earnings per share still increased 17.8% year-over-year from $5.38 in the previous year to $6.34 in fiscal 2025.

For the full year, we still see solid results, but in the fourth quarter the picture is not very strong. Total revenue in the last quarter still increased slightly from $5,251 million in Q4/24 to $5,284 million in Q4/25 - resulting in 0.6% year-over-year top line growth. Adjusted revenue was almost the same - $4,900 million in this quarter, compared to $4,902 million in the previous quarter. Operating income declined 22.6% year-over-year from $1,668 million in the same quarter last year to $1,291 million in this quarter. Diluted earnings per share also declined 7.9% year-over-year from $1.64 in Q4/24 to $1.51 in Q4/25.

Bigger Picture

When looking at the bigger picture, we see Fiserv's growth rates slowing down in the last few years. I am only looking at the last five years, to exclude the acquisition of First Data, which made revenue growth jump in 2020 and distorted the picture.

During these five years, revenue growth rates have constantly been slowing down and were almost zero in the recent past, which is not a good sign for Fiserv. On the other hand, gross margin and operating margin improved in the last few years, which is a good sign for the business. In 2019 and 2020 operating margin declined steeply as a result of the First Data acquisition.

Following the acquisition, margins improved again and operating margin even exceeded pre-acquisition levels. Aside from looking at the trailing-twelve months numbers above, we can also look at the quarterly margins and see Fiserv struggling a bit. Gross margin as well as operating margin are rather declining again - indicating that Fiserv is struggling.

Not only at a company-wide level, we see the business struggling a little bit - this also becomes obvious when looking at the two business segments, in which Fiserv is reporting - Merchant Solutions and Financial Solutions.

Financial Solutions

The Financial Solutions segment is clearly struggling with adjusted revenue declining 2% year-over-year to $2,362 million in Q4/25. Especially adjusted operating margin constantly declined in the last few quarters - from 51.7% in Q4/24 to 42.2% in Q4/25.

Although results are not great at this point, we must point out the long-term contracts with banks and financial institutions, which are making revenue more stable and recurring. Usually this is a good sign and indicating an economic moat around the business and Fiserv still reported good volume growth in debit processing and network volumes. And Zelle, one of the growth drivers for Fiserv in this segment, increased its transactions 15% year-over-year.

Merchant Solutions

The second segment is Merchant Solutions, and this is the segment on which the hopes for higher growth rates mainly rely. In Q4/25, revenue still increased 2% year-over-year to $2,538 million but adjusted operating margin also declined from 39.2% in Q4/24 to 32.1% in Q4/25.

This segment is seeing higher fluctuations for revenue as well as growth rates due to the dependence on payment volume. On the other hand, Clover (which is part of this segment) is seen as one of the growth drivers for Fiserv. Clover is a point-of-sale payment system focusing mostly on small- and mid-size businesses and is generating about $3.3 billion in annual revenue. In fiscal 2025, revenue increased 22%, which is certainly a high growth rate but in Q4/25 revenue growth was only 12% and for fiscal 2026 gross payment volume is expected to grow only in a range of 10% to 15%.

One of the reasons was the Clover fee eliminations that were a 2-point headwind to small business growth. During the last earnings call, management commented:

„Clover volume grew 6% on a reported basis and 9% excluding the previously discussed gateway conversion. Clover volume growth was below our expectations for the quarter, driven largely by softness we experienced in the month of November in the U.S., particularly in the restaurant and retail sectors where we have a large presence. This softness in the U.S. was consistent with broader industry trends and Clover volumes reaccelerated on a combined basis in December and January to approximately 11% ex the gateway conversion."

Growth Potential

The decisive question right now is whether Fiserv is only going through a brief transformative period and can return to higher growth rates again or whether the company's business model is somehow broken and the business as well as the company's stock price must adapt to a new reality.

At this point, it certainly seems like competition is intensifying and almost every payment processing company is struggling. Adyen seems to be under pressure and PayPal is clearly struggling for several years now. And while it seems rather unlikely that all of these companies will lose market share and will struggle, it is certainly possible that fees for example will decline due to market pressures and cheaper technology being available. A rising number of alternatives are also having an impact on the fees a business can charge. With only two companies operating in a market, the fee structure will be different compared to 20 companies offering similar services.

In the end, payments are a commodity and that is a risk these companies are always facing. While Mastercard and Visa were able to charge high fees for decades and have been immensely profitable, other companies might struggle to generate an economic moat around payments. And when looking at Fiserv, we can also raise the question whether the company actually has a wide economic moat around the business.

The stock clearly outperformed the S&P 500 (even including the crash in the last few quarter) but not only return on invested capital is remains below the 10% threshold - especially the First Data acquisition had a very negative impact on RoIC. The company is also reporting rather fluctuating margins raising the question of how wide a moat around the business might be. However, the important gross margin is constantly improving indicating some form of pricing power. In my opinion, we can classify Fiserv's moat as being narrow at best.

Aside from the fees (as percentage of payment volume) it is especially the payment volume that is determining the revenue of these businesses. We can for example look at the Personal Consumption Expenditures and see still solid growth (5.3% right now). Of course, this is including the impact on inflation - but for payment companies, high inflation rates could be rather positive.

But we don't know how long these growth rates will last. Especially when considering the very negative consumer sentiment, there is reason to be cautious.

Nevertheless, management is continuing to be optimistic - especially for Clover. During the last earnings call, management stated:

Consistent with our preliminary view in October and assuming stable macroeconomic conditions, we expect Clover GPV growth of 10% to 15% in 2026 ex the gateway conversion. The lower end represents the core growth rate, while the higher end assumes more significant conversion of non-Clover merchants. Based on these volume expectations, the impact of Clover fee eliminations and more moderate growth from Argentina, we expect Clover revenue to grow in the low double digits for 2026. On a structural basis, our medium-term revenue growth rate target for Clover remains in the 15% to 20% range.

Analysts also seem to be optimistic for the years to come. Between fiscal 2025 and fiscal 2030, earnings per share are expected to grow with a CAGR of 12.77% during these five years. And when excluding fiscal 2030 (which is based only on the opinion of one analyst), earnings per share are still expected to grow with a CAGR of 6.85% in the years until fiscal 2029.

A huge impact on bottom line growth rates in the years to come will stem from share buybacks. Fiserv is not paying a dividend but is spending huge amounts on share buybacks. And since 2005, Fiserv is constantly lowering its diluted shares outstanding - only interrupted by the shares that were issued as part of the First Data acquisition.

Since the beginning of 2020, the company reduced shares with a high pace and the diluted number of shares outstanding declined from 695 million in early 2020 to 537 million in early 2026. This is resulting in a CAGR of 4.2% and in fiscal 2024 and fiscal 2025 the company spent close to $6 billion annually on share buybacks. Management is also indicating that it will continue to use its free cash flow also for share buybacks. During the last earnings call, CFO Paul Todd stated:

Finally, to the extent we generate any excess cash from business and asset optimization activities, we intend to deploy this additional cash to share repurchase.

And considering the extremely low valuation multiples the stock is currently trading for, share buybacks would be extremely impactful right now.

Risky Balance Sheet

While Fiserv can and should use its free cash flow for share buybacks (as the stock seems to be undervalued), we should not ignore the balance sheet and the rather high debt levels. On December 31, 2025, the company had $27,758 million in long-term debt and $1,239 million in short-term debt. When comparing the total debt to the total equity of $25,809 million, we get a D/E ratio of 1.12, which seems acceptable.

However, when comparing the total debt to the free cash flow of the last four quarters (which was $4,341 million), it would take almost 7 times free cash flow to repay the debt and when comparing to the operating income of $5,698 million it would still take 5 times operating income. These are metrics only acceptable for companies growing with an extremely high pace, and therefore have to be seen as risky.

And when looking at the long-term debt, we see debt levels increasing from $23,730 million at the end of fiscal 2024 to $27,758 million at the end of fiscal 2025, which is also not a great sign. When looking at the different years, $3.5 billion in debt mature in 2028 with the other years have lower amounts. We have to assume that Fiserv will be able to repay its debt and won't fave any liquidity issues. However, the high debt levels will restrict Fiserv's ability to make acquisitions or big investments in the years to come.

Intrinsic Value Calculation

In a final step, we must calculate an intrinsic value for Fiserv to determine whether the stock is actually a bargain at this point. We can start by looking at the valuation multiples the stock is currently trading for. At the time of writing, Fiserv is trading for 8.7 times earnings and only for 7.0 times free cash flow. The current valuation multiples are the lowest in almost 30 years (maybe even longer, I don't have data) and of course, it is clearly below the 10-year average metrics (which were 23.46 for P/FCF ratio and 38.35 for the P/E ratio).

Additionally, we are also calculating an intrinsic value by using a discount cash flow calculation. As always, we calculate with a discount rate of 10%, as this is the annual return we like to achieve at least, and we calculate with the last reported diluted number of shares outstanding (537 million in Q4/25).

As basis for our calculation, we could use the free cash flow of the last four quarters, but it could make sense to be a little more cautious. Right now, the free cash flow margin is 20.5% and therefore above the average of 17.8% in the last 20 years. Instead, let's calculate with a free cash flow of $3,772 million (taken the revenue of the last four quarters and the average FCF margin). Now we have to determine a growth rate for the years to come. In a first calculation let's be very cautious and assume that Fiserv's growth period is over and the company will not be able to increase its free cash flow again.

Even in such a very pessimistic scenario, we get an intrinsic value of $70.24 and with a current stock price of $55, it is already trading more than 20% below its intrinsic value.

But let's be a little more optimistic and assume 4% growth for the next few years, which is still below analysts' assumptions and long-term growth rates of Fiserv. It is also the growth rate the company achieved in the last few years solely by share buybacks. When calculating with these assumptions, we get an intrinsic value of $117.07 for the stock - making Fiserv deeply undervalued.

Conclusion

While I see Fiserv deeply undervalued when only looking at valuation multiples or our intrinsic value calculation, we must question whether the company actually has a wide economic moat around its business. It seems like Fiserv is falling into the category "fair company, but wonderful price" and while investments in these companies and stocks can be extremely profitable, we should certainly not ignore the high debt levels the company has on its balance sheet.

Nevertheless, I clearly see Fiserv as a "Buy" at this point as the company will probably be able to grow at least in the low single digits making the stock undervalued. However, we should not necessarily expect a sharp rebound and higher stock prices right away. It is possible that Fiserv will remain at current price levels for several quarters.

data.title: GDX: Waiting For Lower Prices Or Selling Still Makes Sense (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
I remain in a long-term Hold to short-term Sell rating for gold/silver miners generally. We may need one more move lower, perhaps a further -20% to -30% dump in price, before I become more constructive on the sector.

I am largely reiterating my downgrade in February here from Buy for the VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX). The parabolic move from October to January in precious metals is still in correction mode, reasoned from my 40 years of experience watching/trading precious metals.

The next phase of selling may be aligned with a credit contraction in the global financial markets, with U.S. equities falling into bear market territory (usually considered to be a -20% or greater price decline from a peak). In this regard, the 2026 Iran-war spike in crude oil could cause the Federal Reserve to contemplate raising interest rates to protect the U.S. dollar's value and keep a lid on inflation expectations. Wall Street is not priced for rising bond rates, especially on the short end of the yield curve. Gold and silver typically fall hard during credit crunch events.

The good news for gold and silver investors is a major slowdown in economic activity, alongside problems in the bond and equity markets, should eventually lead to another material round of central bank easing monetary policy. However, it may take another 2-3 months or longer to get there.

In the meantime, lots of room exists for a price correction in precious metals after the meteoric rise of 2025. I am targeting a GDX drawdown to $65-75 into early summer.

Short-Term Technical Readout

I have been increasingly worried about precious metals since December, when global exchanges began a series of margin requirement increases. This usually happens at important tops in price, when financial market and government authorities become concerned about economically disruptive speculation by investors.

The late January to March selloff that followed has done tremendous damage to the bullish chart pattern that existed beforehand. Prices have dumped under their 50-day moving averages for starters, with only a feeble rally in recent weeks.

On Balance Volume numbers have faded in early 2026. Plus, the 20-day Chaikin Money Flow reading (a more elaborate oscillator on the Accumulation/Distribution Line) has witnessed clear selling pressure for the first time since early 2025. Both are pictured on the chart below.

Perhaps most problematic is the crash in the 14-day Ease-of-Movement indicator in March to all-time lows (circled in gold). It is basically telling us price was able to drop precipitously on light volume (in an unusual manner). In other words, buying interest has been disappearing.

Assuming gold/silver prices turn down again, the odds of a steep "leveraged" percentage drop in GDX are quite high. How low could the quote fall is a great question. My view is a decline below its 200-day moving average (and 250-day MA not pictured around $72) may be next. Such would generate an unfortunate result of large investment losses since December for the hot money and trend-followers late to the precious metals boom. Many would sell in disgust.

Corroborating the bearish turn lower in GDX, the most popular gold and silver bullion ETF has witnessed a similar implosion in momentum, with record low EMV readings during February and March. I have drawn SPDR Gold Shares (GLD) and iShares Silver Trust (SLV) momentum below, over the same 1-year time scale.

Lastly, the standard 1-year Renko chart design is now highlighting significant price resistance in the $99-100 area for GDX. Volume as a function of price change has all but evaporated vs. a year ago, when heavy volume buying was present. To me, this may be a telling signal of an exhaustion event, where lower prices are now the "path of least resistance."

Final Thoughts

If you can ignore the bullish Wall Street headlines on gold/silver regarding central bank buying and deficits in supply (which is hard to do after a massive 2-year gain), today's chart momentum pattern looks the same or worse than previous peaks in GDX and the gold mining industry.

The huge drop in the Ease-of-Movement indicator is quite concerning, no matter the commodity, ETF, or individual equity you want to review. (I have discussed bearish EMV movements in many Sell-related articles over the past several years.) Overhead resistance to $100 may mean flat to lower GDX pricing will be the future (from $95 currently), maybe for the rest of 2026.

My current estimates of "fair value" for gold of US$3,600 (vs. $4,680 today) and silver of $50 to $55 (vs. $73 now) an ounce do hint at sizable declines as a real possibility soon. My estimates are yearly median averages, where half the time each metal should trade above and half below these calculations. I keep a running number for both monetary metals based on cost-of-production data and comparisons to other commodities and financial assets, plus historical relative relationships to U.S. money supply and Treasury debt growth. This long-term "valuation" idea was one of the main reasons I was so bullish on precious metals during 2024-25.

Of course, there are scenarios where gold/silver and GDX could remain high or even extend their gains in 2026. For example, if China invades Taiwan and increases its purchasing volumes in the gold market while exerting tight export controls on silver to the Western world (which was set up in December), $5000 gold and $100 silver would be sustainable levels, in my view.

However, absent unexpected geopolitical trouble, I do forecast a washout situation in precious metals as the most likely outcome into summertime. If and when dramatically lower prices appear, I plan on adding exposure incrementally. For GDX specifically, I remain in the Hold-to-short-term-bearish camp. It's the logical course of action for me, using independent and experienced analysis.

Thanks for reading. Please consider this article a first step in your due diligence process. Consulting with a registered and experienced investment advisor is recommended before making any trade.

data.title: COWZ: Is Opportunity Knocking Again For This $18B Cash Cows ETF?
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last reviewed the Pacer US Cash Cows 100 ETF (COWZ) on October 31, 2025, when I rated it a "hold" after noting several fundamental deficiencies relative to its main competitor, the VictoryShares Free Cash Flow ETF (VFLO). While COWZ is still trailing VFLO since the latter's launch in June 2023, it has outperformed by about 3.5% since my article was published. For that reason, I wanted to see if anything has changed behind the scenes, which wouldn't be unusual, given COWZ is known for its extremely high portfolio turnover rates.

My take is that although both have extremely strong value and free cash flow characteristics, COWZ has outperformed mainly due to 11% less tech sector exposure. COWZ also slightly overweights Energy, and given that WTI crude oil prices are back above $100 per barrel, I wouldn't be surprised to see COWZ lean on that sector in future rebalances. That mix could lead to the kinds of strong returns COWZ realized four years ago, so while I still don't find its backward-looking selection process appealing, it is an interesting and unique satellite play that earns it a solid "hold" rating. I look forward to explaining why in further detail below, and as always, I welcome your questions in the comments section afterward.

COWZ: Understanding The Strategy

COWZ has a 0.49% expense ratio, $18B in assets, and tracks the in-house Pacer US Cash Cows 100 Index, aiming to hold high free cash flow large/mid-cap stocks trading at attractive valuations. Pacer does a nice job explaining the strategy on its website. As shown below, the process starts with the Russell 1000 Index, narrows down the selection universe to the top 400 based on free float market capitalization, and then removes all companies in the Financials sector as well as those with negative consensus estimated earnings or free cash flow for the next two years. Pacer then selects the top 100 based on free cash flow yield (free cash flow divided by enterprise value) for inclusion in the Index, and then weights them by free cash flow (not free cash flow yield) after applying a 2% security cap.

Even though Pacer has the data available for estimated earnings and free cash flow, they don't use it when selecting and weighting the top 100 stocks. VFLO is different because its Index uses "expected" free cash flow, which is a blend of historical and forward-looking estimates. As to what strategy is better, I've long argued that COWZ's approach means it can take a long time to rotate out of sectors that no longer make sense. That happened in 2023 after inflation had peaked, but at the same time, it can work in some market regimes. COWZ saw a huge surge in AUM in 2022 due to its excellent gains relative to the iShares Russell 1000 ETF (IWB), which signaled to investors that it has the potential to do well in both bull and bear markets.

COWZ Sector Analysis: Setting Up Well For A Slowing Economy Driven By High Oil Prices

Despite excluding Financials from its selection process, COWZ is reasonably well diversified at the sector level. As shown below, its largest two sectors are Health Care (22%) and Technology (18%) followed by Energy and Industrials at 16% and 12%, respectively. However, Utilities exposure is 0%, and due to the limited number of choices in the Russell 1000 Index (only 40) and the capex-heavy nature of the sector that requires constant reinvestment, free cash flow for most companies is usually negative.

Compared to VFLO, COWZ underweights Technology by 11% and overweights Consumer Staples by 9%. That helps to explain why COWZ has held up better YTD (4.24% vs. 1.29%), and certainly, it seems better positioned than VFLO if oil prices remain elevated and the economy slips into a recession. A recent example was in 2022, when Energy (XLE) and Consumer Staples (XLP) held up well, with total returns of 64.17% and -0.83%. Meanwhile, Technology (XLK) returned -27.73%.

Looking ahead, I see Energy becoming a bigger part of the COWZ portfolio. Exposure is at 16.23% right now, but there are several names that could get picked up in future quarterly reconstitutions. My modeling indicates Cheniere Energy (LNG), Chevron (CVX), Exxon Mobil (XOM), and Phillips 66 (PSX) are likely adds once today's high oil prices are reflected in their respective financial statements. Since each would likely command COWZ's max 2% allocation, total exposure could easily exceed 25% by the end of the year.

COWZ Fundamentals: Consistently Good Exposure To Value And Quality Factors

The next table highlights selected fundamental metrics for COWZ's top 20 holdings, which comprise 39.40% of the portfolio. Contributing to its lower risk profile is a 0.82 five-year portfolio beta, which is the weighted average beta of its current holdings. Notice how that's 0.13 points below VFLO and 0.09 points below the iShares Russell 1000 Value ETF (IWD), which is the main benchmark Pacer ETFs uses on its website. At a minimum, that sets COWZ up as a decent diversification tool to help weather current market volatility.

When looking at the summary statistics at the bottom, it's clear that COWZ and VFLO share several similarities. Both have trailing P/E's of around 18x and trailing P/CF ratios of around 8.5-9.0x. Weighted free cash flow margins are also excellent at about 15.50%, and capital efficiency is strong, evidenced by a roughly 13% return on total capital figure. For me, that's exactly what the strategy is designed to offer, especially following quarterly reconstitutions. Investors should get pretty good exposure to the value and quality factors, but it's important to note that this "guarantee" often comes with a cost.

For COWZ, that appears to be growth. Its 1.22% 3Y EPS CAGR doesn't inspire confidence from an earnings growth durability perspective, but in fairness, this growth rate does not reflect the current reality of high oil prices. The Iran War is the clear catalyst, and I don't believe prices will retreat as quickly as President Trump has suggested, even if U.S. involvement wraps up in a few weeks. As a result, the inflation play is back on the table. Per the University of Michigan, short-run inflation expectations jumped to above 4.3% for those surveyed after the start of military action in Iran, while short- and long-run expected business conditions also dipped sharply.

Consequently, there has also been a notable shift in interest rate expectations since the start of the Iran War. One day prior to the conflict, the CME Group's FedWatch Tool indicated a 93% cumulative probability of rate cut between now and the October meeting. Today, that probability is only 5.1%, and there's even now a 13.2% probability of an interest rate hike. Therefore, I see some similarities between 2022 and today, and given its history, COWZ could be a solid tactical inflation hedge as it was four years ago.

COWZ Performance Analysis: A Long-Term Look At Its Return Distribution Curve

What I've described above is using COWZ as a short-term hedge against high oil prices, inflation, and interest rates. However, with $18B in assets under management, there are clearly many who treat it as a long-term holding. You might think that with COWZ, you just have to "trust the process," so with that in mind, let's take a look at how such an investor would have fared had they bought into the fund at its inception. Per the table below, the answer is that COWZ has been the better investment compared to IWD, outperforming it on annualized total returns (12.64% vs. 9.57%) from January 2017 through April 3, 2026.

Still, COWZ hasn't behaved like a classic large-cap value ETF. For instance, its 18.65% annualized standard deviation is elevated and even above the 15.71% figure for IWB. Furthermore, it only delivered positive returns 60.7% of the time (68/112 months) compared to 64.3% (72/112 months) and 70.5% (79/112 months) for IWD and IWB. You can see in the distribution table below how "reliant" it is on rare single-month returns above 10%, which historically were achieved in high-inflation environments.

Also, we shouldn't forget about VFLO. Although its track record is short, it is outperforming COWZ on every key statistic. Since July 2023, it holds the edge on annualized returns (18.80% vs. 12.07%), Sharpe Ratio (0.98 vs. 0.60), and Sortino Ratio (2.00 vs. 0.93). Lastly, its drawdowns tend to be lower and its recoveries quicker, so despite COWZ's recent win, I still feel that VFLO is the better long-term play. Its strategy also mirrors what I initially suggested in my COWZ article from four years ago, when I wrote that "the use of trailing instead of projected cash flows is a flaw."

Investment Recommendation

COWZ is an $18B free cash flow yield-based ETF that has historically done well in high inflation regimes. It became well-known to many investors in 2021-2022 after demonstrating that it could outperform its benchmarks in both bull and bear markets. However, now that the ETF is nine years old, it's clear the strategy requires either the patience of a long-term investor or the discipline of a short-term trader. If you try to play the middle ground, I think there's a decent chance you'll trail benchmarks like IWD and IWBs because COWZ's backward-only approach results in it staying in underperforming stocks and sectors for too long.

Fortunately, I think the short-term outlook for COWZ is mostly strong. Oil prices are back up above $100 for the first time since 2022, and the ETF should benefit more as it leans more into Energy stocks in 2026, as is my expectation. Lastly, its solid quality and value features cut down on speculation risk, so while I still have reservations about the strategy and prefer VFLO in the long run, COWZ certainly earns a solid "hold" rating today. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments below.

data.title: GDX: New Macro Risks, Same Solid Valuation For Gold Mining Stocks
data.description:
Shares of gold mining companies have been on a wild ride already in 2026. The VanEck Gold Miners ETF (GDX) has returned 108% YoY, outperforming the S&P 500 by more than 80 percentage points. It has more than doubled gold’s return. YTD, GDX sports a 10.3% gain, dividends included. Still, the volatile fund is about 20% off its February all-time high. Rising real interest rates have weighed, along with money possibly moving from precious metals to energy commodities amid the war in Iran.

Still, Goldman Sachs recently reiterated that it expects gold to reach $5400 per ounce by year-end 2026. Likewise, I reiterate a buy rating on GDX. While new risks appear—such as higher energy prices—GDX’s valuation is appealing, while the technical situation points to some support on the chart. I was bullish on GDX in January.

GDX Correction, Outperforming the S&P 500 & Gold YoY

Rising Real Yields Stinging Gold

Goldman Sticks With Its Gold Price Target

According to the issuer, GDX seeks to replicate as closely as possible, before fees and expenses, the price and yield performance of the NYSE Arca Gold Miners Index, which intends to track the overall performance of companies involved in the gold mining industry. VanEck states that gold miners have historically provided leveraged exposure to gold prices, and the fund owns the largest global gold mining firms.

GDX is a large ETF with $29 billion in assets under management as of April 3, 2026. That’s up from $26 billion at the time of my previous analysis. Shares are up 8% since then, outperforming the S&P 500 by 12 percentage points. GDX’s annual expense ratio is moderate at 51 basis points, while its trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at just 0.67%.

Share-price momentum is rated well by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system, but I’ll call out later that the technical situation shows some signs of exhaustion. From a risk perspective, GDX has a very high 44% historical standard deviation and a forward implied volatility reading of 57%.

Finally, on liquidity, the ETF scores well. Its average daily volume is north of 30 million shares, while the median 30-day bid/ask spread is just two basis points, per VanEck ETFs.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star ETF by Morningstar plots along the top row and right side of the style box, indicating that most exposure is to global large caps and growth shares. More than three-quarters of GDX is invested in non-US stocks, so there is ample diversification from a country standpoint.

On valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio is very low at just 11.2x. Pair that with a long-term EPS growth rate of 15.1%, and the resulting PEG ratio is below 1. That’s a compelling number. Of course, much hinges on how gold performs, and it has evolved into a risk asset in recent months. So, GDX’s diversification benefit is, for now, somewhat low.

GDX: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

Zooming in on the sector breakdown, the top 10 stocks account for a high 61% of the product. It’s a 100% Materials-sector ETF, so owning it likely results in an active overweight to the resource trade (since Materials is such a low percentage of the All Country World Index).

What’s more, earnings season is on the horizon, so keeping up with both the fundamentals and technicals of the three biggest positions is key.

GDX: Holdings & Dividend Information

From a seasonal take, April has been the best of the 12 months when scanning price returns over the past 10 years. Weaker returns have then developed heading into the end of the first half before an average July rebound.

GDX: Bullish April Trends

The Technical Take

With a still-attractive valuation and bullish near-term seasonals, GDX’s technical situation is mixed. Rising real interest rates have hurt since early in the year, but the chart offers specific clues on price points to watch. Notice in the graph below that the ETF retraced half of the February-March decline. I now see resistance at a gap fill near $95, while the long-term 200-day moving average is on the rise, suggesting that the bulls still control the primary trend. The 200dma comes into view at $78.

But take a look at the rising uptrend line that was broken earlier this year. That means the previous uptrend is over. That break came after a bearish divergence between the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the chart and price. GDX has now ventured into a high-congestion zone, which is supportive. Making me nervous is a downside measured move price target to the October low in the high $60s (based on the early-year range and subsequent breakdown).

GDX: Shares Pause at Resistance, Uptrend Broken, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on GDX. Long the fund myself (but having reduced exposure from September 2025 to December 2025, through donating appreciated shares to my donor-advised fund), gold miners’ collective P/E is low, while the chart points to mixed risks. Macro-wise, investors should monitor real yields, as GDX trades in tandem with risk assets like the S&P 500.

data.title: Check Point: Market Prices Stagnation, But Partial Transition Can Drive Upside
data.description:
Introduction—The Architecture Shift

Check Point Software (CHKP) has a long track record of proactive breach protection. Enterprise customers trust its deterministic approach built over 3 decades of existence. Its firewalls and gateway-based security architecture are standards in the network security industry.

CHKP’s core architecture uses the concept of a gateway. In this dimension, traffic flows through network gateways where stateful inspection enforces policy. Intrusion prevention systems (IPS) and signature-based antivirus detect and block known threats. Originally delivered as Firewall-1, this functionality evolved into CHKP’s Software Blade architecture. Customers trusted this because of its deep packet inspection and broadly high-confidence blocking of known threats before they could execute.

To understand where Checkpoint fell behind, network gateways have lost their primacy as the primary control point. Today, they are just one layer among many. Applications are now delivered via SaaS over HTTPS and are hardly distinguishable from normal web traffic. Many users do not sit behind enterprise firewalls anymore - they operate from unmanaged devices across residential networks. North-south traffic’s primacy has been replaced by a mix of east-west traffic consisting of service-to-service communication via APIs, containers, and microservices. Some of these packets do not traverse a traditional firewall at all.

The concept of security itself has shifted. CrowdStrike’s Falcon Endpoint Detection and Response (EDR) platform now operates directly at the endpoint - not the choke point. The concept is now behavioral analytics, not signatures. Threat detection is now probabilistic, not deterministic. There are cloud-native platforms like Zscaler (ZS) that route traffic through distributed proxies. These platforms rely on a zero trust policy based on identity and context, not network location. Even attack modes have shifted, and the concept of a single-perimeter security has lost primacy.

Check Point has been late in identifying the shift. That is what the company is navigating now.

Today’s Security Approach is Different

Within the Software Blade architecture, traffic was matched against known signatures and policy rules. There was a clear, deterministic logic that was used to block/allow traffic. Three things made this work: traffic was predictable, there were centralized enforcement points/choke points, and threats came with a signature and could be classified.

All three conditions have now weakened. First, we still have TLS inspection. However, with increasing HTTPS encryption, enterprise traffic is less visible now. Second, traffic doesn’t primarily flow through choke points anymore. Especially in cloud-native environments, much of the traffic now flows within Kubernetes clusters, serverless functions, and API gateways. Third, threats are no longer easily classifiable. They are often fileless, use legitimate tools like PowerShell or WMI, use dynamic AI-enabled adaptations, and so on. You cannot easily detect them using a signature-based approach.

Enforcement points have, therefore, shifted. They are now redistributed across distributed edges, SSE/ZTNA layers, and SASE architectures. At the same time, the threat model is now behavioral. Modern attacks do not rely on static payloads, using legitimate tools like PowerShell, WMI, and SSH; operate without files; move using stolen credentials; and AI-generated variants even adapt in real-time. These cannot be reliably stopped using signatures and predefined rules.

In such a fundamental shift, the gateway is just one of many signals - and often not the most important one.

Investment Framing—Where the Market is Mispricing CHKP

Once we agree that Check Point’s legacy architecture is outdated, the next question becomes, can they change? And the more important question—can the market rightly price their ability to change?

Currently, the market values CHKP as a mature, low-growth network security vendor. The market sees ~5-6% revenue growth, a decent 30-40% operating margin, and significant cash generation—and the market thinks “no re-acceleration potential.”

The market could be wrong. First, CHKP is not starting from scratch. If it reinvents itself, it doesn’t need to go all the way in order to be rerated. The transition is not trivial. They need to expand their architecture beyond only network enforcement. That would involve endpoint telemetry, identity-aware access, and cloud-native inspection. This will take multiple years of go-to-market and R&D shift. However, my point is that even a partial execution built on existing competency is enough to change the revenue profile.

Second, there are already subtle signs that this is already happening. Some segments, like the subscription business, are growing at a higher pace than the rest. In Q4, it grew 11% YoY to $325 million. That shows that CHKP is focusing on software-led recurring revenue models. Thirdly, CHKP is also investing in adjacent layers. In their Q4 earnings call, the company noted that they now secure their customer’s AI-enabled business “through 4 strategic solution pillars, each one of them a platform of its own. Hybrid Mesh Network Security, securing the infrastructure; Workspace Security, securing the employees; Exposure Management that provides situational awareness; and then finally, AI Security across all of these pillars.”

Given the multiples it is applying, however, the market is not yet appearing to see any major success in that transition. The market probably thinks expansion will happen within network security only, with only modest growth in adjacent layers and no real change in overall growth trajectory.

This creates an interesting angle. If CHKP remains primarily a network enforcement layer, the current valuation is fine. There’s stable growth, high margins, and strong free cash flow—but no premium valuation.

If the company is able to transition properly, however, their financial outlook will improve sharply. Companies that have modernized their architecture now get higher data ownership and greater pricing power. Endpoint and SASE are driving R&D spending in the network security business, and even a modest expansion in these can improve revenue; however, nothing of this sort is being priced in by the market.

Where CHKP Can Still Win

It is not necessary for Check Point to displace cloud-native leaders like CrowdStrike or Zscaler to succeed. Its one key advantage is its installed base of enterprise firewall customers. Here, they already control enforcement points and policy. They only need to build competence in adjacent layers—bundling cloud security, SASE, and endpoint capabilities into existing deployments. They would need to spend less on customer acquisition costs because they already have customers. They would only need incremental adoptions within existing contracts. There is no need to compete head-on with more advanced players. There are at least two hardware/tech areas where it is possible to expand quickly.

First, one segment for potential expansion is in hybrid networks plus cloud enforcement. CHKP already generates ~$2.7B revenue, growing at ~5-6%, largely from network-led deployments. If they can attain even a little bit of share in the large base of cloud security, that can move the needle. Let’s say they get an additional 3-4% incremental growth from expanding into cloud and hybrid deployments; then the total revenue growth gets into high single digits (~8-10%)—that doesn’t need a huge breakthrough anywhere else.

Second, SASE could be a good place to look into. The global SASE market is growing faster than CHKP’s core business. It doesn’t need to become a leader here; just basic penetration within its installed base is all that’s needed for now. For context, Zscaler has 3,886 customers generating over $100,000 in ARR—so we may say that enterprise SASE deployments typically scale into six-figure annual contracts. CHKP has 100,000 global customers. If even 5–10% of this base adopts SASE over time—equivalent to roughly 5,000–10,000 customers—at a conservative $50,000–$100,000 per deployment annually, this translates to approximately $250M–$1B in incremental revenue. This is material for growth but still does not imply category leadership. Consolidated growth could rise to ~8-13% under such a scenario.

To get there, the math is straightforward:

To get to this growth, there is no need to assume category leadership in any one segment. The key idea is that Check Point does not need to be a leader—just meaningful participation in these growth markets is enough to change its revenue profile as well as its market perception.

Valuation—What is Priced vs. What Changes

To anchor all that technology and theory in hard numbers, let’s start with the current baseline. Currently, CHKP trades at roughly ~13-14x forward earnings (non-GAAP) versus a sector median of ~21x. That is a nearly 35% discount coming from its currently slow, mature 5-6% growth.

Let’s take the base case—the one that’s currently priced in. We start with revenue of ~$2.7B, a multi-year EPS approximation of ~$11.5, we set growth at ~5-6%, and we use a ~13-14x price multiple.

When we project 3-year EPS growth at ~7% CAGR, we get ~$14. Applying the same multiple, we get $196. With some modest re-rating and some buybacks, we can get to $200-$230. This gives us a low-volatility, modest compounding outcome. This is our base case.

Now let’s say partial transition works, revenue growth becomes ~10-12% CAGR, and some decent operating leverage and mix creates a ~13-15% CAGR EPS growth. So at year 3, taking 14% as the midpoint, we get EPS of $17.04.

Now comes the key variable—multiple. We don’t need to assume parity with the sector (~20–25x). Even partial success could expand the multiple to ~16–18x. At 16x, we get ~$272, and at 18x, we get $307 as the valuation. So our bull case range is ~$270-310. This assumption is consistent with how the market values transitioning infrastructure vendors—companies like Cisco, IBM, and Oracle, which continue to generate strong cash flows but trade in a ~18–22x range due to slower growth and legacy exposure. Even in a successful transition, Check Point is more likely to move toward the upper end of that range rather than re-rate to platform-level multiples.

This is an achievable target because we only need an additional 3-4% from SASE/cloud, maybe 2-3% more from hybrid expansion, and 2-3% from subscription mix. That easily doubles the growth without needing any unrealistic assumption like category leadership.

While we are at it, let’s also mathematicize the bear case. This occurs due to structural erosion—not just a failure to achieve relevancy. Assume the downside is not just flat, but it is negative operating leverage. So revenue growth becomes ~0-3%, and EPS growth is ~0% or even slightly negative due to pricing pressure and other structural issues. Now, EPS at year 3 goes down to ~$10–11. Apply a compressed multiple of ~11-12x for what is now essentially no-growth software.

Then the valuation becomes ~$110 at the lower end of that range and ~$132 at the higher end.

What will cause such a scenario? A number of technology missteps - say firewalls increasingly become bypassed, SASE vendors capture traffic directly instead of going through a company like CHKP, or CHKP fails to attach new products. Here we have a slow redundancy scenario, not a complete collapse—just what the bear case says.

To put all that in a table:

Scenario

EPS (3 yr)

Multiple

Value

Bear

$10–11

11–12x

Base

~$14

Bull

$16–17+
