$270–310

This exercise shows us something very important—and positive. The downside does not require a collapse. It can emerge gradually if the gateway becomes obsolete faster than Check Point can replace it with new control layers. That deterioration in renewal rates can directly pressure both revenue and margins. However, even here, the downside is limited to roughly ~10–25%, because the business is supported by the company’s installed base, mission-critical positioning, and high-margin recurring revenue.

On the other hand, the upside can approach 2x over time, even if driven by just a partial success in adjacent layers like SASE and cloud security.

That asymmetry makes CHKP attractive at current levels.

data.title: Devon Energy: Merger Deal, Iran War, Price Tailwinds
data.description:
Devon Energy (DVN) is going to be a major beneficiary, like so many other oil and gas companies, from surging petroleum prices following America's and Israel's attacks on Iranian military installations, starting at the end of February. Although petroleum prices recently dropped on hopes of a peace agreement, U.S. President Trump recently threatened an escalating pace of military strikes on Iran again. As the risk of a prolonged military campaign in the Middle East is looming larger, I see U.S.-focused independent exploration and production companies like Devon Energy as strong investment choices for investors.

More importantly, Devon Energy just a short while ago announced a transformative merger with Coterra Energy, which will allow the energy enterprise to grow its production footprint, diversify more into natural gas, and potentially capture significant merger synergies.

Previous Rating

I rated independent oil and gas company Devon Energy a strong buy in June 2025 -- A Capital Return Play -- following the U.S.'s strike on Iran's nuclear facilities, mainly because of a strong price reaction at the time in petroleum markets. Most importantly, the recent escalation between the U.S. and Iran, which spread the conflict to other Gulf countries and led Iran to block the vitally important Strait of Hormuz, creates significant earnings upside for Devon Energy. I believe shares of Devon Energy are too cheap and have long-term upside revaluation potential, whether or not the U.S. will end its war with Iran in the short term.

Iran War Backdrop Creates Earnings Upside for Devon Energy

Devon Energy is headed for a strong first-quarter earnings report given that petroleum prices have surged to more than $100 per barrel following America's and Israel's combined attack on Iran. The increase in petroleum prices since February 28, 2026, is set to create a major profit windfall for integrated as well as independent exploration and production companies like Devon Energy that are heavily focused on oil production.

Most importantly, the energy enterprise's assets are all located in the U.S., and not in the Middle East, which safeguards the firm's production at a time of increasing supply uncertainty. In the fourth quarter, Devon Energy averaged 390 MBbl/d in oil production vs. the same production volume in the preceding quarter. Including natural gas and NGL production, Devon Energy had an oil-equivalent production of 851 MBOED in the fourth quarter, which continues to come mainly from the core Delaware Basin (~61%).

With the Coterra Energy acquisition (discussed below), however, Devon Energy's total production volume is set to grow at a stronger rate going forward, mainly because it is going to add faster-growing non-oil assets to its production mix.

Merger Deal With Coterra Energy Set to Expand Devon Energy's Operational Footprint

On February 2, 2026, Devon Energy announced a transformative all-stock merger with Coterra Energy, a major natural gas shale company in the U.S., which valued the enterprise at $58.0B, based off of enterprise value. The transaction will allow Devon Energy to combine its oil-heavy Permian assets with Coterra's high-quality Marcellus gas assets, creating a more diversified energy enterprise in the process with cash flow upside related to growing operational scale.

The combined entity is set to have a pro forma production volume of 1.6M barrels of oil equivalent per day, with approximately 48% coming from Coterra Energy. Most importantly, Devon Energy said in its merger announcement that it expects the transaction to be accretive to free cash flow and that it targets $1.0B in annualized cost savings by FY 2027.

Devon Energy's Valuation

Devon Energy has seen a significant repricing to the upside after the outbreak of hostilities in the Middle East at the end of February. Although shares have slightly corrected to the downside at the end of March in hopes of a peace agreement between the U.S. and Iran, signs are pointing to a continued escalation in the military conflict.

Shares of Devon Energy are currently valued at a forward price-to-earnings ratio of 12.2X, which places Devon Energy's shares above the 3-year average price-to-earnings ratio of 10.2X. However, I would hardly say that the energy enterprise is overvalued here, especially not in the context of other energy companies with an upstream business focus.

ConocoPhillips (COP) and Diamondback Energy (FANG) are trading at forward P/E ratios of 18.4X and 13.4X, while EOG Resources (EOG) is priced at 11.6X forward earnings. The industry group average P/E ratio here is 13.9X, which would leave room for Devon Energy to revalue a little further to the upside.

Assuming that the Middle East conflict between the U.S., Israel, and Iran drags on for longer (6 months or more), I would expect that petroleum prices remain elevated, potentially above $100 per barrel. This obviously poses a serious earnings and free cash flow growth catalyst for Devon Energy, which achieves approximately 46% of production from its oil wells.

I believe that Devon Energy could trade at a ~15.0X P/E ratio easily, assuming that the transaction with Coterra Energy closes as expected in the second quarter and merger synergies can be realized. A 15.0X earnings multiplier, applied to a consensus FY 2027 earnings estimate of $4.13 per share, calculates to a fair value of $62 per share... which is about 25% above the firm's current market price.

Risks With Devon Energy

The biggest risk for Devon Energy in the short term is obviously a normalization of the price picture in the crude oil market. A peace agreement between the United States and Iran would likely bring down energy prices a lot, potentially back to pre-war price levels of $70 per barrel. This would almost certainly eradicate Devon Energy's potential for a major profit windfall and lead to a valuation correction. What would change my mind about Devon Energy specifically is if the company failed to close the merger with Coterra Energy or if its merger synergies could not be realized.

Final Thoughts

Devon Energy, as an independent oil and gas exploration company, is a primary beneficiary of rising oil prices. While shares of Devon Energy have repriced higher following the outbreak of a new war in the Middle East in February, the energy enterprise is still trading at a very affordable P/E ratio, most certainly when compared against ConocoPhillips, which is also aggressively growing its production base in the Permian. Devon Energy is now a play on Coterra-related merger benefits and an expansion of its production footprint in the long term, but also higher price realizations in the short term. I like the cheap valuation of Devon Energy's shares and would continue to recommend DVN for energy-oriented growth investors, despite a 42% price gain since my last recommendation.

data.title: S&P 500: Prepare For Change (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Last week's action in the S&P500 (SPY) was again dictated by war-related news stories, and the familiar early week recovery rally happened to be the same size as the early week recovery rallies (in futures) on March 9th and March 23rd. However, the usual mid-week reversal and drop on Thursday recovered, and the weekly close was a notable change. This article looks at how and why the technicals have shifted positive, and what this means for the coming weeks.

S&P500 Monthly

Last Tuesday's rally meant that the March bar did not close dead on the lows, which dulled the immediate downside bias. That said, the bounce was not large enough to save the bar from a bearish pattern and it looks very much like March 2025. The Q1 bar on the quarterly chart also closed with a bearish pattern.

Another doubt stems from the lack of good support at the 6316 low. The 20SMA at 6280ish was not reached, while the major level of 6147 is yet to be tested. Of course, there's nothing to say that major levels have to be touched, but reversing in no man's land is not ideal for a sustainable bottom.

April is typically a bullish month - in fact, it ranks second for performance with an average gain of 1.4% - but that doesn't mean it can't drop first (like last year), or just trade inside the large March range to set up a drop in May.

Resistance is 6775 and 7002.

A DeMARK exhaustion count completed in February and is having an effect.

S&P500 Weekly

The weekly chart underwent a notable change last week as it finally closed higher after four straight weeks of closes at new 2026 lows. This came right on cue as a weekly downside Demark exhaustion signal completed on bar 9 (of 9).

This signal is quite rare as it takes 9 down weeks and SPX corrections are usually much shorter. There was a signal at the 2025 low, which bottomed on bar 8, then at the very low of the 2022 bear market, again on bar 8. Before that, Jan '16 on bar 8, June '12 on bar 9, and March '09 (the crash bottom) on bar 8.

The last time this signal completed and failed to lead to a sustainable bottom was way back in October '08. I suppose that's a comfort but also a warning: if this signal fails, watch out below.

The 6316 low didn't really come at any compelling weekly support, but the rally reclaimed the 50SMA and the 6520-50 lows of the range.

Resistance if 6764 and the 20SMA, which will dip into the same area. 6850ish will also be resistance at the high volume (distribution) level.

S&P500 Daily

Monday's dip tested minor resistance pointed out in last weekend's article at 6343. However, there wasn't any immediate sign of a bullish reaction as the session closed weak, and it was only when Tuesday's session held the opening lows and rallied through the gap at 6453 that we could be more certain of a recovery. Tuesday's bullish session was followed by Wednesday's gap higher out of the channel and Thursday's re-test and higher close. It was the first green Thursday in ten weeks.

Just to muddy the waters slightly, the futures channel, which I consider more important as much of the action has taken place out of hours, did not convincingly break. However, I suspect it breaks next week and the intraday charts have a potential inverse head and shoulders pattern that would trigger on a move above 6609.

Support is 6480 and 6385.

Resistance is at the 200SMA and 50% retrace in the 6640-50 area. After that, 6764 is a major level.

An upside Demark exhaustion signal will be on bar 3 (of 9) on Monday so no reaction is expected next week.

Drivers/Events

The fog of war has not lifted. Trump threatened to bomb Iran into the Stone Age in a TV address on Wednesday, and despite some positive hints from Iran over the course of the week, they have rejected negotiations.

Who was buying into a long weekend when an escalation looked certain, and why?

Whoever it was must have been confident.

On the positive side, there has finally been some increased passage through the Strait. Also, Trump has handed over responsibility for its re-opening and seems prepared to end the war in 2-3 weeks, whether it is open or not. What that means for oil prices remains to be seen, but at least there is an easy "out" for the US.

Friday's jobs report was strong with 178k jobs added, while the unemployment rate dropped to 4.3%. That's probably a bit stronger than the markets would have liked as further rate cuts hinge on a cooling labour market. As Fed Chair Powell said on Monday,

There's sort of downside risk ​to the labor market, which suggests keep rates low, but there's upside risk to inflation, which suggests maybe don't keep rates low.

Remove the downside risk to the labour market, and there is no justification for cuts. I don't believe they will hike, but it is something markets may fret over.

Next week's data highlight will be CPI on Friday. This will certainly jump higher due to the oil price shock, but by how much? Expectations are for the MoM to rise from 0.3% (already high) to 1.0%, and for the YoY to move from 2.4% to 3.4%. EU figures were released last week, and annual inflation rose from 1.9% to 2.5%. The problem is they will likely rise further in the coming months.

Probable Moves

Last week brought some notable technical changes as the Demark exhaustion signal had a clear effect, the cash channel broke, and the weekly chart closed strong. I can't tell what the newsflow will be like ahead of Monday's open, but the market's reaction to any bad news is likely to be less bearish, and I expect higher lows to hold. Indeed, Thursday's overnight low of 6463 has a decent chance of holding.

While a larger recovery should be underway, there are reasons to believe it will fade and set up another leg down. The quarterly/monthly charts don't look like they have bottomed, and the decline did not even reach the 38% Fibonacci retrace of the 2025-2026 bull market. 50-61% remains my expectation.

The 200SMA around 6650 could act as initial resistance next week, but 6764 is the likely destination for this bounce. 6850 is possible, but not as probable.

When this recovery concludes, 6150ish should be the next downside target.

data.title: Wall Street Brunch: Trump Ramps Up Iran Threats
data.description:
Listen below or on the go via Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Trump threatens big Tuesday Iran strikes over Strait deadline. (0:17) Markets brace for hotter inflation. (1:50) Payrolls crush expectations. (2:51)

The following is an abridged transcript:

President Donald Trump intensified his rhetoric toward Iran, threatening major infrastructure strikes if the Strait of Hormuz is not reopened under a 48-hour deadline he set Saturday morning.

On Sunday, Trump posted: “Tuesday will be Power Plant Day, and Bridge Day, all wrapped up in one, in Iran. There will be nothing like it!!! Open the F-----n’ Strait, you crazy bastards, or you’ll be living in Hell - JUST WATCH! Praise be to Allah.”

The not-safe-for-work message — notable for its tone, explicit language, and invocation of Allah on Easter — triggered an immediate uproar across social media.

The post followed one a day earlier in which Trump warned that “all hell” would rain down if the Strait was not reopened.

Later Sunday, in a phone interview with Fox News correspondent Trey Yingst, Trump said negotiations with Iranian officials remain active. But if “they don’t make a deal and fast,” he is “considering blowing everything up and taking over the oil,” according to Yingst’s account.

Prediction markets showed limited movement following the comments.

On Kalshi, the odds of traffic in the Strait returning to normal by May 15 held around 14%, roughly unchanged. Odds of reopening by June 1 rose to 30%, up 7 percentage points.

On Polymarket, the probability of the conflict ending by May 15 ticked up to 28% from 25%, while odds of resolution by June 30 slipped to 55% from 60%.

Meanwhile, OPEC+ members are preparing to approve a modest increase in May oil (USO) (BNO) production targets despite the disruption to flows.

Bloomberg reports that key producers, including Saudi Arabia and Russia, are expected to back a plan to raise quotas by roughly 206K bpd during a virtual meeting.

On the economic front, this week is all about inflation.

On Thursday, the Fed’s preferred inflation gauge — core PCE — arrives alongside consumption data. But February’s numbers may already feel dated given the recent energy surge. Core PCE is expected to rise 0.4% M/M, with the annual rate edging down to 3%.

Friday’s March CPI report will be more closely watched for the early impact of higher oil prices.

Headline CPI is forecast to jump 1% M/M, pushing the annual rate up to 3.4% from 2.4%. Core CPI, which excludes food and energy, is seen rising 0.3% on the month, with the annual rate ticking up to 2.7% from 2.5%.

While core CPI excludes energy directly, higher oil prices can filter through other components — such as airfare — as airlines adjust to rising jet fuel costs.

SA analyst Damir Tokic says if the war drags on, “inflation expectations are likely to de-anchor as inflation becomes more persistent, at which point the Fed will need to start hiking.”

Investors will also get their first full trading reaction to Friday’s March jobs report, released during the market holiday.

Nonfarm payrolls rose 178K, more than triple the 51K consensus estimate. The unemployment rate edged down to 4.3%.

Heather Long, chief economist at Navy Federal Credit Union, noted solid hiring across several industries, but warned that slowing wage growth could “hurt many workers.”

Delta Air Lines (DAL) headlines a thin calendar, with analysts expecting EPS of $0.60 on revenue of about $14.9B. The carrier raised its Q1 revenue outlook in March, citing healthy demand.

SA analyst Tim Dunn notes Delta’s Trainer refinery could save as much as $1B relative to peers amid elevated crack spreads and geopolitical risk.

Investors will also be listening for commentary on TSA staffing pressures during the government shutdown.

Also on the calendar:

Levi Strauss (LEVI) reports Tuesday. Constellation Brands (STZ) and Applied Digital (APLD) join Delta on Wednesday. BlackBerry (BB) weighs in Thursday.

And for income investors, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) goes ex-dividend Monday, with payment scheduled for April 30.

The Gap (GAP) goes ex-dividend Wednesday, paying April 29.

AT&T (T) and Verizon (VZ) go ex-dividend Friday. Both pay May 1.

data.title: Buy 7 S&P 500 April Dividend Dogs
data.description:
Foreword

Introducing his Kiplinger Investing newsletter article, Dan Burrows notes, “Sometimes stocks with the highest dividend yields can be fool's gold. And this could be pertinent to some of the stocks with the highest dividend yields in the S&P 500 today.

Be forewarned that in some cases, the names below have entered elite-yield territory only because their share prices have come under stress. In turn, that has some analysts concerned about the sustainability of payouts going forward.”

While most of this collection of S&P500 dividend stocks over-priced or reveals only skinny dividends, seven of the thirteen highest yield 'safer' S&P500 April Dogs are ready to buy. The list was screened to remove stocks showing negative annual returns this month.

April finds: VICI Properties (VICI); Verizon (VZ); Franklin Resources (BEN); Ford Motor (F); Host Hotels (HST); AT&T (T); KeyCorp (KEY), and Regions (RF) living up to the dogcatcher ideal of annual dividends from $1K invested exceeding single share prices. All seven are also 'safer' because they have free cash flow adequate to support their dividends.

With renewed downside market pressure of up to 20%, all ten of the top 'safer' dividend dogs could become ideal, fair-priced buys, with annual yield (from $1K invested) meeting or exceeding their single share prices.

[See a summary of the top ten fair-priced S&P500 Dogs in Actionable Conclusion 21 near the midpoint of this article.]

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 25.96% To 44.73% Net Gains From Top-Ten S&P500 Dogs By April 2027

Four of the ten top dividend-yield S&P500 divi-dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were also top ten price gainers based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this April, 2026 yield-based forecast for S&P500 dogs, as graded by Wall St. wizard estimates, was 40% accurate.

Estimated dividend returns from $1,000.00 invested in each of the ten highest-yielding S&P500 stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the estimates below. (Note: one-year target prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to April, 2027 were:

BXP Inc. (BXP) was projected to net $447.32, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 22 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 3% greater than the market as a whole.

Alexandria Real Estate (ARE) was projected to net $408.01, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 16 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this 33% greater than the market as a whole.

Amcor (AMCR) was projected to net $340.42 based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 29% less than the market as a whole.

VICI Properties was projected to net $323.18, based on the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, plus the estimated annual dividend, less broker fees. The beta number showed this 30% less than the market as a whole.

International Paper Co. (IP) was projected to net $317.76, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 13 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 14% over the market as a whole.

Skyworks Solutions, Inc. (SWKS) was projected to net $305.72, based on the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 31% over the market as a whole.

Hormel Foods (HRL) was projected to net $294.49 based on the median of target price estimates from 10 analysts, plus dividends, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 64% under the market as a whole.

Invitation Homes Inc. (INVH) was projected to net $285.77, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 19% less than the market as a whole.

Healthpeak Properties (DOC) was projected to net $275.49, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 18 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 10% over the market as a whole.

Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) was projected to net $259.64 based on the median of target prices estimated by 21 analysts, less broker fees. The beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 5% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 32.58% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten S&P500 dividend stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 5% over the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends; (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

18 S&P500 Dogs By Total Return Estimates

This reader-suggested addition to my monthly report ranks the top 18/50 S&P500 top yield stocks by total returns. As the reader commented, “Total returns are the critical screen for many income investors.” To this end, 32 negative returns were not highlighted.

50 S&P500 Dogs By Analyst Target-Gain Estimates

The April, 2026 S&P500 50 By Yield

Actionable Conclusions (11-20): April S&P500 Dividend Stocks By Yield Ranged From 6.25% To 8.91%

The Top ten April S&P500 Dogs represented five of eleven Morningstar sectors.

Five consumer staple stalwarts, placed first, third, fourth, seventh, and ninth: Conagra Brands (CAG) [1], Kraft Heinz (KHC) [3], Campbell's (CPB) [4], General Mills (GIS) [7], and Altria Group (MO) [9].

Meanwhile, two real estate stocks placed second, and sixth: Healthpeak Properties (DOC) [2], and VICI Properties [6].

Fifth place went to the lone industrials member, United Parcel Service (UPS) [5], and eighth place belonged to the materials representative, Amcor [8].

Finally, one technology member captured the tenth slot, HP Inc. (HPQ) [10], to complete the March top ten Dogs of the S&P500 by yield.

Dividend vs Price

The graphic above shows the relative strengths of the top ten S&P500 dogs by dividend and price as of market close 4/1/2026.

In April two of the top-ten S&P500 dogs by yield showed less than ideal status: United Parcel and Altria. The other eight projected annual free cash flow yields (from $1k invested) exceeding their single share prices.

S&P500 Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 4/1/26

The remaining bottom forty on the above chart shows more S&P500 dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

April’s least expensive dividend dog of the S&P500 is Conagra at $11.23 per dividend dollar, and the most expensive of the top 50 is Host Hotels (HST) at $23.95 per dividend dollar. Of all 500, however, Nvidia (NVDA) has the most expensive dividend at $4,587.16.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Fifteen of Fifty Top Yield S&P500 Dividend Dogs Show Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash to pay dividends. The April cash-poor fifteen are: United Parcel, Amcor, Pfizer, Kimberly-Clark, International Paper, Hormel Foods, Mid-America Apartment, AES Corp, Edison International, ONEOK, Simon Property, Eversource Energy, Camden Property, Dominion Energy, and Federal Realty. They are considered unsafe to buy due to dividend yields exceeding their free cash flow yields.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, you must look for opportune pullbacks in price to add to your position and improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pullback opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, wars, uncertainty, deals, trades, and reciprocity, too.

Price Drops or Dividend Increases Could Get All Top 10 S&P500 Dogs Back to "Fair Price" Rates For Investors

The charts above retain the current dividend amount and adjust the share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal or exceed the single share price of each stock. As the top illustration shows, nine of the top ten are already ideally priced. However, United Parcel, is $17.11 outside the ideal zone.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) 21.12% To 40.34% Top Ten S&P500 Dividend Upsides To April 2027; (32), No Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target estimates provided another tool to dig out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 7.00% Disadvantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 S&P500 Dogs To April, 2027

Ten-top S&P500 April-updated yield (dividend/price) results, as verified by YCharts, made the following ranking:

As noted above, top-ten S&P500 dogs selected 4/1/26 by yield, represented five of eleven sectors. (Communication services, consumer discretionary, energy, financials, health care, and utilities, missed the top-ten cut.)

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected The 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield S&P500 Dogs (34) To Deliver 14.95% Vs. (35) 16.08% Net Gains by All Ten Come April 2027

$5,000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten S&P500 Dividend Kennel by yield were predicted by analysts 1-year targets to deliver 7% LESS gain than $5,000 invested in all ten. The eighth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Amcor, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 34.04%.

The five lowest-priced S&P500 top-yield dogs for April were: Conagra; Healthpeak; HP Inc; Kraft Heinz; Campbell's, with prices ranging from $15.52 to $22.38.

Five higher-priced S&P500 top-yield dogs as of April 1 were: VICI Properties; General Mills; Amcor; Altria; United Parcel, with prices ranging from $27.46 to $97.91.

Differentiating five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten utilizes Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market. Plainly, O'Higgins's method also applies to S&P 500 dividend dogs.

Caution is warranted, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the feckless Fed and trade war-fueled slide and the AI bubble gain momentum into 2026, with the exception of a very few “Hot” and “Meme” stocks, watch most of the 50 top return S&P500 Dogs become fairly priced… slowly, surely, or quite suddenly.

Afterword

Buy Any of Seven IDEAL 'Safer' S&P500 April DiviDogs; Watch Many More

In late March 2026, 14 of the top 54 S&P500 Dividend Dogs with positive one-year returns were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed free-cash-flow yields greater than dividend yields. YCharts 4/1/26 data showed seven IDEAL specimens.

How S&P500 Safer Dividend Vs. Price Results Could Produce A "Fair Price” Results For Investors

The chart at the top above reveals that five dividends from $1K invested in these top ten 'safer' dividend S&P500 dogs conform to the dogcatcher standard of being equal to (or less than) the price of a single share. Those five are VICI Properties, Verizon, Franklin Resources, Ford, and Host, still standing after surviving the 'safer' dividend gauntlet.

The middle chart shows the recent dividend amount, and the bottom chart adjusts that share price to produce a yield (from $1K invested) to equal the single share price of each stock, a so-called “fair price.”

A market correction of just 24% is required to put all 10 “safer” survivors above or equal to their dividend payouts from $1k invested.

There are, of course, other ways for dividends from $1K invested to exceed the price of a single share of stock. That would be for the boards of directors of these companies to raise dividends or create stock splits to bring prices to the dogcatcher ideal level. That's a lot to ask with all the corporate funds doing so much “good” elsewhere.

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Dogs of the S&P500 updates, out here, in public, away from the paywalled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

Footnotes

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

The 7 IDEAL Dogs of the S&P500
